scenario: Cobalt Energy, a utility, redesigned its Camunda 8.8 outage-response process across 600 incidents per day: the old exclusive gateway and service tasks A and B are removed and replaced by a single new service task C. The active instances are currently waiting on service task A and must continue on the new service task C.
question: Which mapping instruction lets the active service task A continue on the new service task C after migration?

options.text: A single mapping instruction from source element id A to target element id C, so that the active service task A is migrated onto the new service task C in the redesigned target process.
A mapping instruction from source element id A to target element id C combined with one from element id B to element id C, in order to keep both source service tasks alive after migration.
A mapping instruction from source element id B to target element id C; only the inactive sibling task may be remapped to a new replacement service task during a process instance migration.
A mapping instruction from source element id A to target element id C_user_task; the migration tool will widen the type from service task to user task during the active instance migration.

a.text: Correct. A single mapping instruction from source element id A to target element id C migrates the active service task A onto the new service task C in the target process, which is exactly the documented pattern for this redesign.

b.text: Incorrect. Element B is inactive and is not present in the target; mapping it adds no value, and only the active element instance of A needs a mapping to C in this scenario.

c.text: Incorrect. Mapping instructions migrate active element instances; the inactive sibling task B does not need a mapping, but the active task A does.

d.text: Incorrect. Mapping cannot change the element type, so an active service task can only be mapped to another service task such as C; widening to a user task is not allowed.

explanation: Active element instances can be migrated to a new target element by providing a source-to-target mapping instruction, provided both elements are of the same type, so service task A maps cleanly to service task C.
